title: Duality for Rabinowitz-Floer homology
speaker: Alex Oancea
affiliation: Institut de Mathématiques de Jussieu-Paris Rive Gauche
abstract: I will explain a duality theorem with products in Rabinowitz-Floer homology. This has a bearing on string topology and explains a number of dualities that have been observed in that setting. Joint work in progress with Kai Cieliebak and Nancy Hingston.

proof_stages.label: String-topology motivation and puzzles
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up based and free loop spaces, the Pontryagin, Chas-Sullivan, and Goresky-Hingston operations, and isolates the domain asymmetry, secondary nature, and Sullivan relation as puzzles to be explained symplectically.

proof_stages.label: Rabinowitz-Floer homology as boundary symplectic homology
proof_stages.summary: For an exact Liouville domain, Rabinowitz-Floer homology is defined using Hamiltonians pinned to zero at the boundary and mixed inverse/direct action limits, with the order of limits chosen so the pair-of-pants product makes it a unital ring.

proof_stages.label: Duality theorem and Poincare-dual reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts a canonical unital ring isomorphism between boundary symplectic homology and cohomology, and ordinary Floer Poincare duality rewrites the cohomology side as homology for the inverted hat Hamiltonian.

proof_stages.label: Acyclic zig-zag cone comparison
proof_stages.summary: A zig-zag Hamiltonian contains the V and hat complexes with differentials only from the V part to the hat part, so deformation to a linear Hamiltonian makes the cone acyclic and yields the homotopy equivalence underlying duality and the induced product.

proof_stages.label: Secondary pair-of-pants product on cohomology
proof_stages.summary: The cohomology product is described intrinsically by a one-parameter family of pair-of-pants operations whose boundary terms factor through continuation maps to a Hamiltonian with zero complex, making the secondary operation a genuine chain map of the required shifted degree.

proof_stages.label: Cone formalism and multiplicative second proof
proof_stages.summary: Viewing Rabinowitz-Floer homology as homology of cones of continuation maps, with inclusion cones for homology and projection cones for cohomology, gives chain-homotopy equivalent cones and a multiplicative second proof of the duality theorem.

proof_stages.label: Cotangent-bundle identification with extended loop homology
proof_stages.summary: For the unit cotangent disk bundle, Viterbo-type identifications place free-loop homology, free-loop cohomology, and the unit cotangent bundle contribution inside the Rabinowitz-Floer complexes, leading to extended loop homology and cohomology as unital rings related by the duality theorem.

proof_stages.label: Reduced loop rings solve the string-topology puzzles
proof_stages.summary: Factoring the continuation map through the zero-energy contribution and the Euler characteristic defines reduced loop homology and cohomology, embeds the Chas-Sullivan and Goresky-Hingston products into the extended ring, turns Sullivan's relation into associativity, and explains the critical-value and sphere-block symmetries by duality.

examples.name: Two based-loop classes on S^n
examples.note: Great-circle families based at a point give classes x and y=x^2, making the Pontryagin product and richness of based-loop homology concrete without being used in the Rabinowitz-Floer duality proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly constructs the two based-loop families on S^n using a base point, tangent vector, equator/planes, then identifies their degrees and relation x, y=x^2

examples.name: Free loop space of an odd sphere via round geodesics
examples.note: The Morse-Bott picture of free loops on an odd sphere, with blocks indexed by iterated great circles and elevator classes such as theta, illustrates the homology/cohomology symmetry later explained by extended loop homology.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the odd sphere’s free loop space via the round energy functional, identifying constant loops, closed-geodesic critical blocks, sample classes/products, and the theta ‘elevator’ between blocks

passes.reason: The free loop space is analyzed using the round odd sphere, whose great-circle geometry is maximally regular and symmetric, as a model example.

examples.name: TQFT-type gluing comparison for Sullivan's relation
examples.note: The comparison with ordinary TQFT gluing relations clarifies that Sullivan's relation is not a standard cobordism-identification relation, preparing for its later explanation via associativity in the extended product.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states and contrasts Sullivan’s relation with a TQFT-type gluing relation, but does not carry out a concrete gluing computation or derive the relation in detail
